name: Trust & Framing

industry_framings.name: Telecom customer support at Helix Communications

central_tension: Perception versus reality in customer-facing AI
background_copy: Customer-facing AI has a quiet asymmetry: what users believe about the AI shapes outcomes as much as what the AI actually does. Frame it as a smart assistant and people forgive the misses; frame it as a search box and they punish the same misses. The research shows the gap between perception and reality is itself a managed variable — it can be widened deliberately (marketing) or narrowed deliberately (calibration), and both moves have second-order costs. You play the AI deployment lead at Helix Communications, a mid-tier telecom rolling out an AI assistant in customer support. Four quarters. One year to choose how aggressively to manage the gap — and to discover what the year teaches you about the kind of marketer (or operator) you are.

anchor_sources.citation: McKinley, S., et al. (2026). The Labelling Effect: How AI Framing Shapes User Trust. Information Systems Research.
anchor_sources.contribution: Foundational paper. Establishes the labelling-as-framing mechanic.

anchor_sources.citation: Yu, K., et al. (2025). Trust Calibration Under Model Opacity. AI & Society.
anchor_sources.contribution: Documents how miscalibration accumulates as accumulating user mistrust.

anchor_sources.citation: Gillespie, N., et al. (2025). Transparency, AI, and Organisational Trust. Journal of Management Studies.
Esmaeilzadeh, P. (2024). Adoption Dynamics for Customer-Facing AI. Decision Support Systems.
anchor_sources.contribution: Establishes the adoption-curve mechanics behind the adoption bricks.

anchor_sources.citation: Rosenthal, M., & Iqbal, A. (2025). Hype Collapse: When Framing Snaps. MIS Quarterly.
anchor_sources.contribution: Documents the hype-collapse pattern that motivates the Q3 public-incident event.

bricks.label: Launch transparency plan
bricks.briefing_description: Publishes a transparency plan. Modest perception lift; adoption ticks up.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) — transparency as a perception lever.

bricks.label: Reframe as smart assistant
bricks.briefing_description: Reframes the product as a smart assistant. Big perception + adoption lift. Real performance unchanged.
bricks.research_note: McKinley et al. (2026) — labelling shifts perception without changing capability.

bricks.label: Add transparency disclosure
bricks.briefing_description: Adds a transparency disclosure to the UI. Honest move; perception and adoption fall back.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) — transparency narrows the gap by pulling perception down.

bricks.label: Aggressive launch campaign
bricks.briefing_description: Runs an aggressive launch campaign. Biggest perception + adoption lift. Sets the hype clock.
bricks.research_note: Rosenthal & Iqbal (2025) — aggressive framing builds the hype that later collapses.

bricks.label: Quiet rollout
bricks.briefing_description: Rolls out quietly with no marketing push. Perception and adoption stay low; no hype to manage.
bricks.research_note: McKinley et al. (2026) inverse — absence of framing is itself a framing choice.

bricks.label: Train AI on more data
bricks.briefing_description: Trains the model on a fresh batch of customer interactions. Real performance + small adoption.
bricks.research_note: Standard supervised-learning improvement; foundational to Yu et al. (2025).

bricks.label: Add safety filters
bricks.briefing_description: Adds safety filters to AI responses. Real performance up; adoption slips from over-refusals.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) on safety vs adoption tension.

bricks.label: Improve response speed
bricks.briefing_description: Cuts response latency in half. Real performance + adoption.
bricks.research_note: Esmaeilzadeh (2024) on latency as adoption driver.

bricks.label: Add domain expertise
bricks.briefing_description: Adds telecom-domain training. Biggest real-performance lift; small adoption + board lifts.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) on domain specificity.

bricks.label: Quiet performance update
bricks.briefing_description: Pushes a performance update without announcing it. Real-performance gain; no perception movement.
bricks.research_note: McKinley et al. (2026) — silent improvement narrows gap without consuming hype.

bricks.label: Publish accuracy data
bricks.briefing_description: Publishes accuracy data quarterly. Perception drops as users calibrate; board approves.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) — accuracy disclosure narrows perception toward reality.

bricks.label: Run user expectations survey
bricks.briefing_description: Runs a structured user-expectations survey. Small calibration moves all around.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) on calibration mechanics.

bricks.label: Train agents on AI limits
bricks.briefing_description: Trains agents to set AI limit expectations with customers. Calibration move with adoption cost.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) on human-mediated calibration.

bricks.label: Internal critical training
bricks.briefing_description: Runs internal training emphasising the AI's known limitations. Strongest calibration move.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) on internal-culture calibration.

bricks.label: Calibration scorecard
bricks.briefing_description: Stands up a calibration scorecard tracked at exec. Small perception move; big board signal.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) — measurement as governance.

bricks.label: Push to second BU
bricks.briefing_description: Expands deployment to a second business unit. Big adoption lift.
bricks.research_note: Esmaeilzadeh (2024) on scope expansion as adoption driver.

bricks.label: Manager incentive program
bricks.briefing_description: Ties manager bonuses to AI adoption. Solid adoption lift.
bricks.research_note: Esmaeilzadeh (2024) on incentive-driven adoption.

bricks.label: Customer-facing pilot
bricks.briefing_description: Runs a customer-facing pilot. Largest adoption lift.
bricks.research_note: Esmaeilzadeh (2024) on customer-facing pilot effect; Rosenthal & Iqbal (2025) hype build-up.

bricks.label: Power-user community
bricks.briefing_description: Stands up a power-user community. Steady adoption + perception lifts.
bricks.research_note: Esmaeilzadeh (2024) on community-driven adoption.

bricks.label: AI ethics board
bricks.briefing_description: Stands up an AI ethics board. Big board signal; small perception + adoption costs.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) on governance signal.

bricks.label: Permanent calibration team
bricks.briefing_description: Stands up a permanent calibration team. Calibration becomes institutional.
bricks.research_note: Yu et al. (2025) institutionalised calibration.

bricks.label: Quarterly accuracy report
bricks.briefing_description: Commits to quarterly accuracy reporting. Structural calibration commitment.
bricks.research_note: Gillespie et al. (2025) — public reporting cadence.

bricks.label: Issue clarification
bricks.briefing_description: Issues a public clarification of what the AI does and doesn't do. Big perception reset.
bricks.research_note: Rosenthal & Iqbal (2025) — clarification as gap-correction.

bricks.label: Reframe entire pitch
bricks.briefing_description: Reframes the product's pitch entirely. Painful but durable correction.
bricks.research_note: Rosenthal & Iqbal (2025) — controlled reframing as preferable to incident.

1.label: Q1 · HYPE SIGNAL
1.text: Press picks up the launch with adjectives you didn't choose. Internal team smiles. Calibration team flags it.

1.label: Q1 · INVISIBLE GAIN
1.text: Real performance is ahead of perception. Customer satisfaction surveys lag the actual numbers.

1.label: Q1 · CLEAN LAUNCH
1.text: Perception and reality tracking together. The board's quarterly review is short and quiet.

1.label: Q1 · LAUNCH FATIGUE
1.text: Customers aren't trying it. Internal champions losing energy.

1.label: Q1 · STATUS
1.text: Quarter passes. Marketing wants more lift. Calibration team wants more honesty.

2.label: Q2 · MISMATCH SIGNAL
2.text: Support tickets reveal expectation mismatch. Press starts to ask harder questions.

2.label: Q2 · UNDERSOLD
2.text: Performance is real but the market doesn't know it. Adoption suffers. Board notices.

2.label: Q2 · ORGANIC TRACTION
2.text: Adoption climbs without the marketing push. Word-of-mouth working.

2.label: Q2 · CALIBRATION CRISIS
2.text: Internal calibration team escalates. The gap is now the public story.

2.label: Q2 · STATUS
2.text: Mid-year. Numbers stable. Pressure building beneath them.

3.label: Q3 · PUBLIC INCIDENT
3.text: Viral thread captures the AI hallucinating confidently. The framing collapses. One life lost.

3.label: Q3 · MISSED WINDOW
3.text: The performance was there; nobody knew. Competitor announces a similar product to applause.

3.label: Q3 · HONESTY DIVIDEND
3.text: Board responds to the calibration discipline. Trade press writes about the realism.

3.label: Q3 · SCALE, NO HYPE
3.text: Big adoption, small gap. The model just works. Boring story; great quarter.

3.label: Q3 · STATUS
3.text: Quarter closes. Year-end packet drafts begin.

quarter_wrap_rules.label: BOARD CONFIDENCE COLLAPSE
quarter_wrap_rules.text: Emergency board call. You spend it defending the framing rather than running the product.

options.label: Publish transparency report
options.description: Publish a public transparency report. The calibrator's move.

options.label: Continue current framing
options.description: Stay the course. Hold the year you've built.

options.label: Relaunch with new framing
options.description: Reset the gap by rebranding. Painful but corrective.

archetypes.label: The Drifter
archetypes.reveal_text: You launched it but didn't manage it. The framing settled where it settled. So did you.

archetypes.label: The Communicator
archetypes.trigger: abs_gap < 15 AND calib_count >= 4 AND board_conf >= 50 AND lives_lost <= 1
archetypes.reveal_text: You kept perception and reality close. The product is what you said it is. The board respects the discipline. The CMO is restless.

archetypes.label: The Marketer
archetypes.reveal_text: You sold the AI ahead of its capabilities. Adoption is real; the gap is real too. Year Two is the year that gap either narrows on its own or breaks.

archetypes.label: The Hype Collapser
archetypes.reveal_text: You built the hype, the framing collapsed, and the year became a case study you didn't want to be in. The product still works. The story doesn't.

archetypes.label: The Quiet Operator
archetypes.trigger: gap < -10 AND actual_performance >= 60 AND adoption < 50 AND lives_lost <= 1
archetypes.reveal_text: Real performance is ahead of perception. Customers don't know what you built. The CFO is asking when the adoption catches up to the work.

archetypes.label: The Drifter
archetypes.reveal_text: Your year didn't shape a story. The framing drifted. The product drifted. The pattern is the absence of one. Year Two needs a posture. You're choosing whether to be the one who picks it.
